title: 6.c35 Assignment 1: Data, Visualization, and Housing
description: Designed three visualization sketches to examine how the age and sex structure of the U.S. population changed between 1900 and 2000. Explored population pyramids, block diagrams, and line plots to compare demographic composition, growth patterns, and age-specific trends.
story: This project started with a simple question: how did the structure of the U.S. population change over a century? By sketching multiple visualization styles, I explored how design choices shape what patterns become visible. The process highlighted how the same dataset can tell very different stories depending on how it is represented.

title: 6.c35 Assignment 2: Exploratory Visual Analysis
description: Conducted exploratory analysis of zoning and housing patterns across Metro Boston using maps, histograms, and comparative charts. Investigated how single-family zoning relates to household composition, income distribution, and racial diversity.
story: Housing policy often appears abstract, but its effects shape real neighborhoods and communities. Through exploratory analysis of zoning data in Metro Boston, I examined how single-family zoning relates to demographic and economic patterns. The work revealed how uneven and complex these relationships are across municipalities.

title: 6.c35 Assignment 3: Visualization Critique and Redesign
description: Critiqued a spiral visualization of COVID-19 case trends and redesigned it to improve interpretability and analytical clarity. Developed alternative designs including time-series, heatmap, and bubble visualizations to better communicate pandemic dynamics.
story: A striking spiral chart of COVID-19 cases inspired this redesign exercise. While visually compelling, the original graphic made precise comparisons difficult, prompting a deeper exploration of how form affects interpretation. By iterating through multiple redesigns, I focused on balancing narrative storytelling with analytical clarity.

title: 6.c35 Assignment 4: Persuasive/Deceptive Visualization
description: Created paired visualizations arguing for and against a proposition about abortion clinic availability and abortion rates. The project examines how framing, layout, and metric selection influence interpretation and ethical persuasion in data visualization.
story: This project explored the ethical boundary between persuasion and deception in data visualization. By constructing two opposing visual arguments from the same dataset, I examined how choices like titles, color, and layout subtly guide interpretation. The exercise reinforced how visualization design carries responsibility for how data is understood.

title: Abscess Drain Tracking Database
description: Developing a clinical database and analysis pipeline to track abscess drain placements, complications, and outcomes in interventional radiology. The system supports longitudinal monitoring and research on drainage effectiveness and patient recovery.
story: In clinical practice, abscess drains are placed frequently, yet outcome tracking is often fragmented across systems. This project focuses on building a structured database that captures procedure details, complications, and follow-up outcomes. By organizing this information, the goal is to enable clearer clinical insights and support future research.

title: Deep Vein Thrombosis Management Database
description: Building a structured dataset to study treatment patterns and outcomes for patients with deep vein thrombosis. The project aims to support clinical research and improve decision-making in vascular and interventional care.
story: Deep vein thrombosis management involves many clinical decisions, from anticoagulation strategies to procedural interventions. This project organizes patient data to better understand how different treatment approaches relate to outcomes. The goal is to create a foundation for more data-informed care in vascular medicine.

title: ToolUniverse
description: Developed a platform for organizing and exploring specialized tools and resources across technical domains. The project focuses on improving discoverability and accessibility for researchers and developers.
story: Researchers and developers often rely on a growing ecosystem of specialized tools, but discovering the right one can be surprisingly difficult. ToolUniverse was designed to make that exploration easier by organizing tools into a navigable and searchable system. The project explores how interface design can help people move through complex technical landscapes.

title: GAMI - CHW LLM
description: Designing a large language model assistant to support community health workers in low-resource settings. The system aims to help workers access medical guidance, document cases, and navigate care pathways more efficiently.
story: Community health workers often operate with limited clinical resources while serving as the first point of contact for patients. This project explores how language models could support their work by providing guidance, documentation assistance, and decision support. The aim is to build tools that strengthen frontline care rather than replace it.

title: pH-Sensitive Enzyme Delivery Device for Hyperoxaluria
description: Researching a pH-responsive star-shaped gastrointestinal device that delivers enzymes to degrade dietary oxalate before absorption. The project evaluates device performance and enzyme stability through in vivo testing.
story: Hyperoxaluria can lead to severe kidney complications when oxalate accumulates in the body. This project investigates a soft robotic device designed to travel through the gastrointestinal tract while protecting and delivering oxalate-degrading enzymes. By combining materials science, bioengineering, and in vivo testing, the work explores new approaches to targeted therapeutic delivery.
